factors.summary: The company has pricing power in certain segments, but it is being impacted by reduced demand and supply chain disruptions.

evidence.text: The majority of the Aerospace segment’s operating costs relates to new aircraft production on firm orders and consists of labor, material, subcontractor and overhead costs. The costs are accumulated in production lots, recorded in inventory and recognized as operating costs at aircraft delivery based on the estimated average unit cost in a production lot.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company faces reduced demand and supply chain issues, impacting its pricing power.

factors.summary: Revenue mix is shifting towards lower-margin segments due to production delays and reduced demand in aerospace.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is decelerating due to the impact of COVID-19 on operations and demand.

evidence.text: Our consolidated revenue and operating earnings decreased in the first quarter of 2020 driven by delays in aircraft deliveries in our Aerospace segment due to the COVID-19 outbreak.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: COVID-19 impact led to decreased revenue and earnings, showing growth deceleration.

factors.summary: Forward guidance has been adjusted downward for the year due to the impact of COVID-19.

evidence.text: For the year, we had anticipated delivery of somewhat in excess of 150 Gulfstream aircraft. Due to supply chain disruptions and efficiency impacts resulting from our efforts to comply with CDC protocols, we have adjusted our production rate to 125-130 deliveries. Based on the reduction in deliveries and the anticipated impact of travel restrictions on the volume of aircraft services, we now expect the Aerospace segment’s 2020 revenue to be around $8.5 billion with operating earnings of approximately $1.15 billion.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Forward guidance has been adjusted downward due to COVID-19 impacts.

evidence.text: The company took actions in the first quarter to strengthen its liquidity and financial condition by issuing $4 billion of fixed-rate notes and renewing credit facilities.

sentiment.rationale: Management is cautious but taking actions to mitigate COVID-19 risks.

evidence.text: The United States and other governments have taken a number of steps to respond to the outbreak and to support economic activity and liquidity in the capital markets.

sentiment.label: negative

evidence.text: We will continue to assess further potential consequences to our employees, business, supply chain and customers, and take actions to mitigate adverse outcomes.

sentiment.label: positive

factors.summary: Demand trends for aerospace products are mixed, with some segments showing stability and others experiencing significant declines due to the COVID-19 outbreak. The pandemic has led to a deceleration in demand, particularly noticeable in the last two weeks of the quarter when order activity for new aircraft largely ceased.

evidence.text: Order activity for new aircraft was progressing well until the last two weeks of the quarter, when activity largely ceased due to the COVID-19 outbreak.
Despite this, orders in the first quarter of 2020 reflected demand across our product and services portfolio, and the segment’s book-to-bill ratio (orders divided by revenue) was 1.1-to-1.
Order activity for new aircraft was progressing well until the last two weeks of the quarter, when activity largely ceased.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant declines in demand due to COVID-19 impact.

factors.summary: Backlog orders remain stable, with the total backlog showing minimal change from December 2019 to March 2020. New orders in the defense segments outpace revenue, indicating sustained demand despite the impact of COVID-19, which has been relatively minor for these businesses.

evidence.text: Our total backlog, including funded and unfunded portions, was $85.7 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2020 compared with $86.9 billion on December 31, 2019.
evidence.subsection: BACKLOG AND ESTIMATED POTENTIAL CONTRACT VALUE

evidence.text: The impact of COVID-19 on our defense segments has not been material. While there is some modest pressure on revenue based on our experience to date, at current levels the impact is not sufficient to affect our full-year earnings for these businesses. Therefore, the full-year outlook for our defense businesses remains unchanged.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Stable backlog with sustained demand despite COVID-19.

factors.summary: The end market momentum for the Aerospace segment is negative due to a significant decline in demand caused by the COVID-19 outbreak. Meanwhile, other defense segments, such as Combat Systems, are stable or slightly increasing, with evidence of higher revenue from U.S. military vehicles and stable order activity before the outbreak disrupted the market. Overall, the momentum is mixed across the different segments, with aerospace facing challenges while defense segments remain resilient.

evidence.text: Order activity for new aircraft was progressing well until the last two weeks of the quarter, when activity largely ceased due to the COVID-19 outbreak. Typically, the last few weeks of a quarter experience the most order activity. Despite this, orders in the first quarter of 2020 reflected demand across our product and services portfolio, and the segment’s book-to-bill ratio (orders divided by revenue) was 1.1-to-1.
Revenue from U.S. military vehicles increased due primarily to higher volume on the U.S. Army’s Abrams M1A2 System Enhancement Package Version 3 (SEPv3) tank program.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Negative momentum in Aerospace due to COVID-19, while defense segments are stable.

factors.summary: The company faces significant near-term risks primarily related to the COVID-19 outbreak, particularly impacting the Aerospace segment. These risks include supply chain disruptions, reduced demand, potential economic downturns, and uncertainties around government defense budgets and funding. While no immediate impairment is indicated, the disruptions could affect the recoverability of assets and the segment's performance.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 outbreak has caused significant disruptions to national and global economies, which has impacted our businesses. As of the end of the quarter, we have not identified a triggering event requiring an impairment test for our goodwill, intangibles or other long-lived assets. In our Aerospace segment, which has experienced a more significant impact from the outbreak, we do not believe the impact represents a longer-term change that would indicate that the carrying value of the segment's intangibles and long-lived assets may not be recoverable or that the Aerospace reporting unit's estimated fair value has been significantly affected.

The supplier disruptions along with efficiency impacts resulting from our efforts to comply with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) protocols in our facilities have impacted our productivity. In addition, while we continue to work closely with suppliers to mitigate COVID-19 impacts, some suppliers could experience additional delays.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant near-term risks from COVID-19, including supply chain and economic downturns.

factors.summary: The contract backlog remains stable overall, with slight fluctuations between specific periods. The funded and unfunded portions of the backlog reflect ongoing defense programs, contributing to the consistent outlook for the defense businesses.

evidence.text: Our total backlog, including funded and unfunded portions, was $85.7 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2020 compared with $86.9 billion on December 31, 2019.
evidence.subsection: BACKLOG AND ESTIMATED POTENTIAL CONTRACT VALUE

evidence.text: The full-year outlook for our defense businesses remains unchanged.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Overall stable contract backlog with consistent outlook.

factors.summary: Government defense budgets are stable, with consistent funding directed toward the company's core program areas. However, there is some uncertainty regarding the future funding levels, as the unfunded portion of the backlog may not be fully realized.

evidence.text: The total backlog in our defense segments represents the estimated remaining sales value of work to be performed under firm contracts. The funded portion of total backlog includes items that have been authorized and appropriated by the U.S. Congress and funded by customers, as well as commitments by international customers that are approved and funded similarly by their governments. The unfunded portion of total backlog includes the amounts that we believe are likely to be funded, but there is no guarantee that future budgets and appropriations will provide the same funding level currently anticipated for a given program.

The DoD has issued guidance on managing the impacts of COVID-19 on defense contracts, such as requests for equitable adjustments and other contractual mechanisms to address cost, schedule and performance impacts on contracts.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Stable government defense budgets with some uncertainty.

factors.summary: International sales are stable with several contract wins, though there are potential risks such as geopolitical factors, trade restrictions, and disruptions due to travel restrictions in certain regions.

evidence.text: In the first quarter of 2020, we received the following significant contract awards during the first quarter of 2020: •$300 from the U.S. Army to upgrade Abrams tanks to the M1A2 SEPv3 configuration. •$225 from the Army to provide spare parts and inventory management services for the Stryker wheeled combat-vehicle program. •$145 from the Army to provide systems technical support for Abrams main battle tanks. •$135 to produce Eagle wheeled combat vehicles for Germany. •$90 from the Army for various munitions and ordnance.
Internationally, our defense business has experienced site closures in some countries.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: International sales are stable with contract wins, but risks like geopolitical factors and disruptions exist.

factors.summary: Capital expenditures are stable, with a slight increase and a commitment to future growth. However, the business environment section highlights liquidity strengthening actions rather than direct capital expenditure plans, indicating a mixed outlook.

evidence.text: Cash used for investing activities was $177 in the first three months of 2020 compared with $187 in the same period in 2019. Our investing activities include cash paid for capital expenditures and business acquisitions; purchases, sales and maturities of marketable securities; and proceeds from asset sales. The primary use of cash for investing activities in both periods was capital expenditures. Capital expenditures were $185 in the first three months of 2020 compared with $181 in the same period in 2019.
The company took actions in the first quarter to strengthen its liquidity and financial condition by issuing $4 billion of fixed-rate notes and renewing credit facilities.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Capital expenditures are stable with slight increases, but the focus is on liquidity rather than direct growth investments.

factors.summary: The company's debt leverage has shown mixed trends, with an increase in net debt from $11 billion to $12.7 billion. However, the company has also demonstrated stability through successful refinancing and liquidity management, as evidenced by the issuance of $4 billion in fixed-rate notes and the renewal of credit facilities.

evidence.text: Our net debt position, defined as debt less cash and equivalents and marketable securities, was $12.7 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2020 compared with $11 billion at the end of 2019.
evidence.subsection: FINANCIAL CONDITION, LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

evidence.text: The company took actions in the first quarter to strengthen its liquidity and financial condition by issuing $4 billion of fixed-rate notes and renewing credit facilities.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Debt has increased, but the company has managed it through refinancing and liquidity actions.

factors.summary: Economic conditions are negatively impacted by the COVID-19 outbreak, serving as a significant challenge and headwind for business operations and growth.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 outbreak has caused significant disruptions to national and global economies, which has impacted our businesses.

In our Aerospace segment, which has experienced a more significant impact from the outbreak, we do not believe the impact represents a longer-term change that would indicate that the carrying value of the segment’s intangibles and long-lived assets may not be recoverable or that the Aerospace reporting unit’s estimated fair value has been significantly affected.
The ongoing outbreak of Coronavirus (COVID-19) has caused significant disruptions to national and global economies.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Economic conditions are negatively impacted by the COVID-19 outbreak, causing significant disruptions.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are showing mixed trends, with increased buyback activity and dividend payments contributing to higher returns, while the company's stable dividend and limited buyback increases indicate a more cautious approach to returning value.

evidence.text: In the first three months of 2020, we repurchased 3.4 million of our outstanding shares for $501. On March 4, 2020, our board of directors authorized management to repurchase up to 10 million additional shares of the company’s outstanding stock. On March 4, 2020, our board of directors declared an increased quarterly dividend of $1.10 per share, the 23rd consecutive annual increase. Cash dividends paid were $295 in the first three months of 2020 compared with $268 in the same period in 2019.
The company took actions in the first quarter to strengthen its liquidity and financial condition by issuing $4 billion of fixed-rate notes and renewing credit facilities.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Increased buyback activity and dividend payments indicate a positive approach to returning value.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is mixed, with deterioration due to increased operating working capital and reduced aircraft deliveries, but stability is noted through effective management and liquidity actions taken during the first quarter.

evidence.text: Cash used for operating activities was $666 in the first three months of 2020 compared with $795 in the same period in 2019. Cash flows in both periods were affected negatively by net growth in operating working capital (OWC), particularly our position in the development, production and cash collection cycles of our Gulfstream aircraft models in our Aerospace segment. In the first quarter of 2020, cash flows were also impacted negatively by fewer aircraft deliveries and reduced aircraft orders due to the COVID-19 outbreak.
The company took actions in the first quarter to strengthen its liquidity and financial condition by issuing $4 billion of fixed-rate notes and renewing credit facilities.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Free cash flow has deteriorated due to various factors, but stability is noted through management actions.
